The commission would make all official determinations, and would keep the official list which would be open to all Canadians to view. What we call SkyTrain in BC, is 1) a proprietary railway which patents are owned by Bombardier and SNC Lavalin and 2) a truncated heavy rail metro (Canada Line) which phony P-3 consortium is headed by SNC Lavalin.

The ALRT/ART SkyTrain lines are not considered legitimate rapid transit and only 7 such systems have been built in 40 years under various names;, ICTS. ALRT, ALM, ART.

Due to small platform size and small trains, the ART/ALRT Lines are legally only allowed to carry up to 15,000 pphpd. The Canada line, with station platforms half the length of the ALRT/ART Lines effectively has half the capacity.

The cost to increase capacity on the ALRT/ART Lines - $3 billion; on the Canada Line $1.5 billion.

Today, modern LRT can carry more customers at a much cheaper cost (see Ottawa).

Because of contractual obligations and political chicanery, Metro Vancouver can only afford to build a short ALRT/ART/EMU line every decade. These are Metro Vancouver's rapid transit facts.

I have puzzled over Evertz (ET) since I first looked at it early last year. It has a compelling story ( an inventive techie and a finance guy acquire a company a couple of decades ago and make an interesting business of it), and it has a high R&D budget and quite strong free cash flow (FCF) generation. But it has had little EPS and FCF growth in the last decade. Total returns from 2006 to 2016 were about 7% a year. ROE has fallen from 40% to 50% in 2007 to 2009 to 15% to 20% in recent years. 

A simple Gordon model (sum of current yield and future EPS growth) give me expected returns of 5% a year. I would look for 15% + for small cap that could get disrupted (disruption may already be part of the story).

I also looked at a perpetual FCF at average recent amounts and got a yield of about 5% at today’s price.

Am I missing something here?

Each and every day, 10 men go to a restaurant for dinner together. The bill for all 10 comes to $100 each day. If the bill were paid the way we pay our taxes, the first four would pay nothing; the fifth would pay $1; the sixth would pay $3; the seventh $7; the eighth $12; the ninth $18. The 10th man – the richest – would pay $59. Although the 10 men didn’t share the bill equally, they all seemed content enough with the arrangement – until the restaurant owner threw them a curve.

“You’re all very good customers,” the owner said, “so I’m going to reduce the cost of your daily meal by $20. I’m going to charge you just $80 in total.” The 10 men looked at each other and seemed genuinely surprised, but quite happy about the news.

The first four men, of course, are unaffected because they weren’t paying anything for their meals anyway. They’ll still eat for free. The big question is how to divvy up the $20 in savings among the remaining six in a way that’s fair for each of them.

If the answers were obvious, they already would have been done. The savings aren't obvious--but the potential is still huge and could be identified if we looked at every department of government and asked: 
1) What programs are really necessary and which are not?  
2) Can necessary programs be handled more efficiently by the private sector? 
3) For those that should remain public sector, where is there waste in processes? (activities which add no value such as unnecessary reports, excessive meetings, duplicative activities, etc.)
4) Should public employees contribute more to their healthcare like those in the private sector do? 
5) Can overtime be eliminated? 
6) Etc..

The reality is that many would oppose such a process, because government inefficiency creates more jobs, union dues, overtime bonuses, and thus bigger pensions after retirement.

The “King, Bing, Thing” of 1926 confirms the wisdom of the constitutional convention that a GG/LG should (with very rare exception) act as advised by his/her First Minister where that First Minister has lost a Vote of Confidence at the first opportunity after a General Election; The corollary being that the GG/LG by convention is owed such advice by the First Minister in order that he/she can properly carry our his/her role in clear accord with constitutional precepts.

Why is this important? Answer: There few formal limits that prevent a GG/LG acting today as would have been the practice 200 years ago (i.e as an autocrat). Constitutional conventions, however, have evolved to reflect & and give form & substance to the current democratic nature of our system of Responsible Government.

It is thus the duty of a First Minister, wherever possible, to take political responsibility & not leave a GG/LG hanging without the requisite advice where a difficult decision must be made.

The only proper conclusion that can be drawn from the data in this article is that women (collectively) earn less than men (collectively).  This data says nothing about unfair pay practices and you cannot draw any conclusions on this question.

There are good policy reasons to look at data such as this, either to gauge societal income dynamics, or to gauge fairness of pay, but we have to be very careful to infer that women are discriminated against in terms of income when all else is equal.  This study tries to break it down more, but even fails to go far enough.  For instance it reports 95% wage parity in "Health", but this doesn't account for the different roles in "health" and their pay.  For example, in a Dr. office with equal numbers of male/female doctors each paid the same, the "female average income" will be brought down if you factor in the predominately female lower paid support staff (they tend to be overwhelmingly female).
